   733‚Üí  def container_runtime
   734‚Üí    @container_runtime ||= begin
   735‚Üí      runtimes = Docker.info["Runtimes"] || {}
   736‚Üí      if runtimes.key?("sysbox-runc")
   737‚Üí        "sysbox-runc"
   738‚Üí      else
   739‚Üí        Rails.logger.warn("SandboxManager: sysbox-runc not available, falling back to runc (Docker-in-Docker will not work inside sandboxes)")
   740‚Üí        "runc"
   741‚Üí      end
   742‚Üí    end
   743‚Üí  end
   744‚Üí
   745‚Üí  # mkdir_p with self-healing: if EACCES, fix the parent dir's ownership
   746‚Üí  # via a short-lived Docker container (only way when running as non-root
   747‚Üí  # inside a container with Docker socket access).
   748‚Üí  def ensure_dir(path)
   749‚Üí    FileUtils.mkdir_p(path)
   750‚Üí  rescue Errno::EACCES
   751‚Üí    docker_chown(File.dirname(path))
   752‚Üí    FileUtils.mkdir_p(path)
   753‚Üí  end
   754‚Üí
   755‚Üí  # Ensure a bind-mounted directory is world-writable so the sandbox user
   756‚Üí  # (non-root inside the Sysbox container) can write to it.
   757‚Üí  def prepare_bind_mount(path)
   758‚Üí    stat = File.stat(path)
   759‚Üí    return if stat.mode & 0o777 == 0o777
   760‚Üí    return if system("/usr/bin/sudo", "-n", "/usr/bin/chmod", "777", path)
   761‚Üí    docker_chmod(path, "777")
   762‚Üí  rescue Errno::ENOENT
   763‚Üí    # directory disappeared ‚ÄĒ race condition, ignore
   764‚Üí  end
   765‚Üí
   766‚Üí  # Fix ownership and permissions of a host path via sudo (preferred) or a busybox container fallback.
   767‚Üí  def docker_chown(path)
   768‚Üí    return if system("/usr/bin/sudo", "-n", "/usr/bin/chown", "#{Process.uid}:#{Process.gid}", path) &&
   769‚Üí             system("/usr/bin/sudo", "-n", "/usr/bin/chmod", "755", path)
   770‚Üí    docker_run_fix(path, "sh", "-c", "chown #{Process.uid}:#{Process.gid} /mnt && chmod 755 /mnt")
   771‚Üí  end
   772‚Üí
   773‚Üí  def docker_chmod(path, mode)
   774‚Üí    docker_run_fix(path, "chmod", mode, "/mnt")
   775‚Üí  rescue Docker::Error::DockerError => e
   776‚Üí    Rails.logger.warn("docker_chmod(#{path}) failed: #{e.message}")
   777‚Üí  end
   778‚Üí
   779‚Üí  def docker_run_fix(host_path, *cmd)
   780‚Üí    image = fix_image
   781‚Üí    c = Docker::Container.create(
   782‚Üí      "Image" => image, "Cmd" => cmd,
   783‚Üí      "HostConfig" => { "Binds" => [ "#{host_path}:/mnt" ] }
   784‚Üí    )
   785‚Üí    c.start
   786‚Üí    result = c.wait(30)
   787‚Üí    exit_code = result&.dig("StatusCode") || -1
   788‚Üí    unless exit_code == 0
   789‚Üí      raise Error, "docker_run_fix failed (exit #{exit_code}) for #{host_path}: #{cmd.join(' ')}"
   790‚Üí    end
   791‚Üí  ensure
   792‚Üí    c&.delete(force: true) rescue nil
   793‚Üí  end
   794‚Üí
   795‚Üí  # Pick an image guaranteed to be on this Docker daemon.
   796‚Üí  # Prefer busybox (tiny), fall back to alpine, then any local image.
   797‚Üí  def fix_image
   798‚Üí    %w[busybox:latest alpine:latest].each do |img|
   799‚Üí      begin
   800‚Üí        return img if Docker::Image.get(img)
   801‚Üí      rescue Docker::Error::DockerError
   802‚Üí        next
   803‚Üí      end
   804‚Üí    end
   805‚Üí    # Last resort: use any image already present
   806‚Üí    all = Docker::Image.all
   807‚Üí    raise Error, "No local images available for docker_run_fix" if all.empty?
   808‚Üí    tags = all.first.info["RepoTags"]
   809‚Üí    tags&.first || all.first.id
   810‚Üí  end
   811‚Üí
   812‚Üí  # Inject SMB password into the container via exec, avoiding env var leakage.
   813‚Üí  def set_smb_password(container, user)
   814‚Üí    return unless user.smb_password.present?
   815‚Üí
   816‚Üí    username = user.name
   817‚Üí    password = user.smb_password
   818‚Üí    container.exec(
   819‚Üí      ["bash", "-c", "printf '%s\\n%s\\n' \"$0\" \"$0\" | smbpasswd -a -s \"$1\" 2>&1 || echo 'Warning: smbpasswd failed' >&2",
   820‚Üí       password, username]
   821‚Üí    )
   822‚Üí  rescue Docker::Error::DockerError => e
   823‚Üí    Rails.logger.warn("set_smb_password failed for #{user.name}: #{e.message}")
   824‚Üí  end
   825‚Üí
   826‚Üí  def volume_binds(user, sandbox)
   827‚Üí    binds = []
   828‚Üí    if sandbox.mount_home
   829‚Üí      binds << "#{DATA_DIR}/users/#{user.name}/home:/home/#{user.name}"
   830‚Üí    end
   831‚Üí    if sandbox.data_path.present?
   832‚Üí      host_path = "#{DATA_DIR}/users/#{user.name}/data/#{sandbox.data_path}".chomp("/")
   833‚Üí      binds << "#{host_path}:/persisted"
   834‚Üí    end
   835‚Üí    # Chrome profile persistence: mount separately if not mounting full home
   836‚Üí    if user.chrome_persist_profile? && !sandbox.mount_home
   837‚Üí      host_path = "#{DATA_DIR}/users/#{user.name}/chrome-profile"
   838‚Üí      binds << "#{host_path}:/home/#{user.name}/.config/google-chrome"
   839‚Üí    end
   840‚Üí    binds
   841‚Üí  end
   842‚Üíend
   843‚Üí

1‚Üí# Docker-in-Docker (DinD) Troubleshooting with Sysbox
     2‚Üí
     3‚Üí## Background
     4‚Üí
     5‚ÜíEach Sandcastle sandbox runs inside a **Sysbox** container (`sysbox-runc` runtime). Sysbox makes Docker-in-Docker safe by virtualising the kernel interface, allowing an inner Docker daemon (`dockerd`) to run inside the container without privileged mode.
     6‚Üí
     7‚ÜíHowever, starting with **Docker Engine 29.x**, a subtle ownership bug in how Sysbox creates backing directories breaks DinD on every fresh container. This document explains the root cause, how to diagnose it, and how the permanent fix works.
     8‚Üí
     9‚Üí---
    10‚Üí
    11‚Üí## Architecture: How Sysbox DinD Storage Works
    12‚Üí
    13‚Üí```
    14‚ÜíHost filesystem
    15‚Üí‚ĒĒ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ /sandcastle/sysbox/docker/
    16‚Üí    ‚ĒĒ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ <container-id>/          ‚Üź Sysbox creates this dir (owned root:root)
    17‚Üí        ‚ĒĒ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ ...                   ‚Üź inner /var/lib/docker contents
    18‚Üí
    19‚ÜíInside sandbox container
    20‚Üí‚ĒĒ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ /var/lib/docker               ‚Üź Sysbox bind-mounts the backing dir here
    21‚Üí    ‚ĒĒ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ overlay2/ ...             ‚Üź Docker writes container layers here
    22‚Üí```
    23‚Üí
    24‚ÜíSysbox uses **user namespace isolation**: container UID 0 (root) maps to a non-privileged host UID (the "UID offset", typically 231072). This is what makes running Docker inside a container safe ‚ÄĒ the container thinks it's root, but the host sees it as an unprivileged user.
    25‚Üí
    26‚Üí---
    27‚Üí
    28‚Üí## Root Cause: The Ownership Bug
    29‚Üí
    30‚Üí### What Sysbox Does (Incorrectly)
    31‚Üí
    32‚ÜíWhen a sandbox container starts, Sysbox creates the backing directory:
    33‚Üí
    34‚Üí```
    35‚Üí/sandcastle/sysbox/docker/<container-id>/
    36‚Üí```
    37‚Üí
    38‚ÜíIt creates this directory owned by **`root:root` (host UID 0)**.
    39‚Üí
    40‚Üí### What This Means Inside the Container
    41‚Üí
    42‚ÜíBecause container UID 0 ‚Üí host UID 231072, the container sees this directory as owned by `nobody:nogroup` (UID 65534 or similar). Container root **cannot access** a directory owned by a different host user.
    43‚Üí
    44‚Üí### Why Docker 29 Made It Fatal
    45‚Üí
    46‚ÜíPrior to Docker 29, `dockerd` would attempt `chmod /var/lib/docker` at startup and silently tolerate a permission denied error. Docker 29.2.1 hardened this check and made the `chmod` **fatal**:
    47‚Üí
    48‚Üí```
    49‚ÜíError starting daemon: error initializing graphdriver: error creating overlay mounts
    50‚Üíto start container: operation not permitted
    51‚Üí```
    52‚Üí
    53‚ÜíThe daemon exits immediately. Every new sandbox starts with a broken Docker daemon.
    54‚Üí
    55‚Üí---
    56‚Üí
    57‚Üí## Diagnosis
    58‚Üí
    59‚Üí### Symptoms
    60‚Üí
    61‚Üí1. `docker ps` inside a sandbox fails:
    62‚Üí   ```
    63‚Üí   Cannot connect to the Docker daemon at unix:///var/run/docker.sock. Is the docker daemon running?
    64‚Üí   ```
    65‚Üí2. SSH login shows:
    66‚Üí   ```
    67‚Üí   DinD: FAILED ‚ÄĒ run 'docker-restart' or check /var/log/dockerd.log
    68‚Üí   ```
    69‚Üí3. `/var/log/dockerd.log` contains:
    70‚Üí   ```
    71‚Üí   Error starting daemon: error initializing graphdriver: ... operation not permitted
    72‚Üí   ```
    73‚Üí
    74‚Üí### Confirming the Root Cause
    75‚Üí
    76‚ÜíOn the **host** (sandman), find the container ID and check the backing dir:
    77‚Üí
    78‚Üí```bash
    79‚Üí# Find backing dir for a specific container
    80‚ÜíCONTAINER_ID=$(docker ps --filter name=sc-<sandbox-name> --format '{{.ID}}')
    81‚Üíls -la /sandcastle/sysbox/docker/$CONTAINER_ID
    82‚Üí```
    83‚Üí
    84‚ÜíIf it shows `root root`, the bug is confirmed:
    85‚Üí
    86‚Üí```
    87‚Üídrwx------ 7 root root 4096 Jan 15 12:34 /sandcastle/sysbox/docker/abc123def456
    88‚Üí```
    89‚Üí
    90‚Üí### Quick Manual Fix (for a running container)
    91‚Üí
    92‚Üí```bash
    93‚Üí# On the host (sandman):
    94‚ÜíCONTAINER_ID=$(docker ps --filter name=sc-<sandbox-name> --format '{{.ID}}')
    95‚Üísudo chown 231072:231072 /sandcastle/sysbox/docker/$CONTAINER_ID
    96‚Üí
    97‚Üí# Then inside the sandbox:
    98‚Üídocker-restart
    99‚Üí```
   100‚Üí
   101‚Üí---
   102‚Üí
   103‚Üí## The Permanent Fix
   104‚Üí
   105‚ÜíThe fix lives in `installer/templates/dockyard.sh` (injected into `installer.sh` at build time). A **background watcher** runs inside the Dockyard daemon process and watches for new backing directories created by Sysbox, chowning them to the correct UID within ~1 second of container creation.
   106‚Üí
   107‚Üí### How the Watcher Works
   108‚Üí
   109‚Üí```mermaid
   110‚ÜísequenceDiagram
   111‚Üí    participant D as Dockyard daemon
   112‚Üí    participant W as DinD ownership watcher
   113‚Üí    participant S as Sysbox
   114‚Üí    participant C as Sandbox container
   115‚Üí
   116‚Üí    D->>W: Start background loop (every 1s)
   117‚Üí    Note over D,W: Reads SYSBOX_UID_OFFSET from /etc/subuid
   118‚Üí
   119‚Üí    C->>S: docker run (create sandbox)
   120‚Üí    S->>S: mkdir /sandcastle/sysbox/docker/<id> (owned root:root)
   121‚Üí
   122‚Üí    W->>W: Scan /sandcastle/sysbox/docker/*/
   123‚Üí    W->>W: Find dir owned by uid 0
   124‚Üí    W->>S: chown 231072:231072 /sandcastle/sysbox/docker/<id>
   125‚Üí
   126‚Üí    C->>C: entrypoint.sh starts dockerd
   127‚Üí    Note over C: /var/lib/docker now accessible
   128‚Üí    C->>C: dockerd starts successfully
   129‚Üí```
   130‚Üí
   131‚Üí### Key Implementation Details
   132‚Üí
   133‚Üí**Reading the actual UID offset** (not hardcoding 231072):
   134‚Üí
   135‚Üí```bash
   136‚ÜíSYSBOX_UID_OFFSET=$(awk -F: '$1=="sysbox" {print $2; exit}' /etc/subuid 2>/dev/null || echo 231072)
   137‚Üí```
   138‚Üí
   139‚Üí`/etc/subuid` contains:
   140‚Üí```
   141‚Üísysbox:231072:65536
   142‚Üí```
   143‚Üí
   144‚ÜíThis reads the correct offset even if the system assigns a different range.
   145‚Üí
   146‚Üí**Fixing leftover dirs** (e.g. from before the fix was deployed):
   147‚Üí
   148‚Üí```bash
   149‚Üífind "$SYSBOX_DOCKER_DIR" -maxdepth 1 -mindepth 1 -uid 0 \
   150‚Üí    -exec chown "${SYSBOX_UID_OFFSET}:${SYSBOX_UID_OFFSET}" {} \; 2>/dev/null || true
   151‚Üí```
   152‚Üí
   153‚ÜíThis runs once at Dockyard startup and catches any dirs from containers that were created before the watcher existed.
   154‚Üí
   155‚Üí**Background watcher loop:**
   156‚Üí
   157‚Üí```bash
   158‚Üí(
   159‚Üí    while true; do
   160‚Üí        for d in "${SYSBOX_DOCKER_DIR}"/*/; do
   161‚Üí            [ -d "$d" ] || continue
   162‚Üí            uid=$(stat -c '%u' "$d" 2>/dev/null) || continue
   163‚Üí            [ "$uid" = "0" ] && \
   164‚Üí                chown "${SYSBOX_UID_OFFSET}:${SYSBOX_UID_OFFSET}" "$d" 2>/dev/null
   165‚Üí        done
   166‚Üí        sleep 1
   167‚Üí    done
   168‚Üí) &
   169‚Üí```
   170‚Üí
   171‚ÜíThe loop runs every 1 second. CPU overhead is negligible (a single `stat` call per existing container per second).
   172‚Üí
   173‚Üí### Self-Healing in entrypoint.sh
   174‚Üí
   175‚ÜíThe sandbox `entrypoint.sh` also has a two-attempt startup to handle the race condition where the watcher hasn't yet run when the container first boots:
   176‚Üí
   177‚Üí```
   178‚ÜíContainer starts
   179‚Üí    ‚ĒĒ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ Attempt 1: start dockerd ‚Üí wait 15s
   180‚Üí          ‚Ēú‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ Success: write "ready" ‚Üí done
   181‚Üí          ‚ĒĒ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ Failure: wait 5s more (watcher runs), retry
   182‚Üí                ‚Ēú‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ Success: write "ready (recovered)" ‚Üí done
   183‚Üí                ‚ĒĒ‚ĒÄ‚ĒÄ Failure: write "FAILED ‚ÄĒ run docker-restart" ‚Üí done
   184‚Üí```
   185‚Üí
   186‚ÜíThe 5-second wait gives the host watcher time to chown the backing dir. After the watcher runs, the second attempt succeeds.
   187‚Üí
   188‚Üí---
   189‚Üí
   190‚Üí## The `docker-restart` Command
   191‚Üí
   192‚ÜíA `docker-restart` command is installed in every sandbox at `/usr/local/bin/docker-restart`. Use it when DinD is broken:
   193‚Üí
   194‚Üí```bash
   195‚Üí# Basic restart (stop, restart dockerd)
   196‚Üídocker-restart
   197‚Üí
   198‚Üí# Full reset (also wipes all inner images and containers)
   199‚Üídocker-restart --reset
   200‚Üí```
   201‚Üí
   202‚Üí`--reset` uses `find /var/lib/docker -mindepth 1 -delete` (not `rm -rf /var/lib/docker`) because `/var/lib/docker` is a Sysbox BTRFS bind-mount point ‚ÄĒ the directory itself cannot be removed, only its contents.
   203‚Üí
   204‚Üí---
   205‚Üí
   206‚Üí## Technical Notes
   207‚Üí
   208‚Üí### Why Not Use `--data-root` to Avoid the Problem?
   209‚Üí
   210‚ÜíAttempts to use `--data-root=/var/lib/docker-data` (on the container's overlay layer) failed:
   211‚Üí
   212‚Üí```
   213‚Üífailed to mount overlay: invalid argument
   214‚Üí```
   215‚Üí
   216‚Üíoverlay2 on top of overlay (the container's root filesystem) is not supported even with Sysbox. The inner Docker daemon **must** use the BTRFS-backed `/var/lib/docker` mount that Sysbox provides.
   217‚Üí
   218‚Üí### Why Not Fix It in entrypoint.sh Alone?
   219‚Üí
   220‚ÜíThe container cannot chown its own `/var/lib/docker` backing directory ‚ÄĒ from inside the container it's already owned by the container's root. The host-level backing path (`/sandcastle/sysbox/docker/<id>`) is invisible from inside the container. Only a process running on the **host** can fix the ownership.
   221‚Üí
   222‚Üí### Sysbox Version
   223‚Üí
   224‚ÜíThis behavior was observed with **Sysbox 0.6.7** on **kernel 6.17**. The backing dir ownership issue may be a Sysbox bug that gets fixed in a future release, at which point the watcher becomes a harmless no-op.
   225‚Üí
   226‚Üí---
   227‚Üí
   228‚Üí## Files Involved
   229‚Üí
   230‚Üí| File | Role |
   231‚Üí|------|------|
   232‚Üí| `installer/templates/dockyard.sh` | Source of truth for the watcher (edit this, not `installer.sh`) |
   233‚Üí| `installer.sh` | Generated from template ‚ÄĒ contains the watcher at startup |
   234‚Üí| `images/sandbox/entrypoint.sh` | Two-attempt dockerd startup with 5s recovery wait |
   235‚Üí| `images/sandbox/docker-restart.sh` | Manual recovery tool for sandbox users |
   236‚Üí| `images/sandbox/Dockerfile` | Installs `docker-restart` into the image |
   237‚Üí

490‚Üí    local CACHE_DIR="${SCRIPT_DIR}/.tmp"
   491‚Üí
   492‚Üí    # Version compatibility notes ‚ÄĒ do not upgrade these without reading:
   493‚Üí    #
   494‚Üí    # DOCKER_VERSION: static binary from download.docker.com/linux/static/stable.
   495‚Üí    #   Uses sysbox-runc as default runtime ‚Üí the bundled runc 1.3.3 is never
   496‚Üí    #   called for sandbox containers, so this version does NOT trigger the
   497‚Üí    #   sysbox procfs incompatibility (nestybox/sysbox#973).
   498‚Üí    #
   499‚Üí    # SYSBOX_VERSION: 0.6.7.10-tc is a patched fork (github.com/thieso2/sysbox)
   500‚Üí    #   that adds --run-dir to sysbox-mgr, sysbox-fs, and sysbox-runc, allowing
   501‚Üí    #   N independent sysbox instances per host (each with its own socket dir).
   502‚Üí    #   SetRunDir() calls os.Setenv("SYSBOX_RUN_DIR", dir) and os.Args is scanned
   503‚Üí    #   directly in init() ‚ÄĒ bypasses urfave/cli v1 so --run-dir via runtimeArgs
   504‚Üí    #   works correctly for all three sockets including the seccomp tracer.
   505‚Üí    #   No wrapper script needed.
   506‚Üí    #   Fixed: https://github.com/thieso2/sysbox/issues/5
   507‚Üí    #   Distributed as a static tarball (no .deb, no dpkg dependency).
   508‚Üí    #   0.6.7.10-tc is the first release with an aarch64 static tarball.
   509‚Üí    #   NOTE: 0.7.0.1-tc has a netns regression ‚ÄĒ do not upgrade until fixed
   510‚Üí    #   (see https://github.com/thieso2/sysbox/issues/9)
   511‚Üí
   512‚Üí    # --- Detect CPU architecture ---
   513‚Üí    local ARCH
   514‚Üí    ARCH=$(uname -m)
   515‚Üí    case "$ARCH" in
   516‚Üí        x86_64|aarch64) ;;
   517‚Üí        *) echo "Unsupported architecture: $ARCH" >&2; exit 1 ;;
   518‚Üí    esac
   519‚Üí    echo "  arch:        ${ARCH}"
   520‚Üí    echo ""
   521‚Üí
   522‚Üí    local DOCKER_VERSION="29.2.1"
   523‚Üí    local DOCKER_ROOTLESS_VERSION="29.2.1"
   524‚Üí    local SYSBOX_VERSION="0.6.7.10-tc"
   525‚Üí    local SYSBOX_TARBALL="sysbox-static-${ARCH}.tar.gz"
   526‚Üí    local COMPOSE_VERSION="2.32.4"
   527‚Üí
   528‚Üí    # SHA256 checksums ‚ÄĒ must match exactly; cache hits are also verified
   529‚Üí    # (protects against cache poisoning and mirror tampering)
   530‚Üí    local DOCKER_SHA256 DOCKER_ROOTLESS_SHA256 SYSBOX_SHA256 COMPOSE_SHA256
   531‚Üí    case "$ARCH" in
   532‚Üí        x86_64)
   533‚Üí            DOCKER_SHA256="995b1d0b51e96d551a3b49c552c0170bc6ce9f8b9e0866b8c15bbc67d1cf93a3"
   534‚Üí            DOCKER_ROOTLESS_SHA256="8c7b7783d8b391ca3183d9b5c7dea1794f6de69cfaa13c45f61fcd17d2b9c3ef"
   535‚Üí            SYSBOX_SHA256="9107dca08cc69c5871a0be7981dec3a3e8e5aa6e0924b7a6ca36df324357274b"
   536‚Üí            COMPOSE_SHA256="ed1917fb54db184192ea9d0717bcd59e3662ea79db48bff36d3475516c480a6b"
   537‚Üí            ;;
   538‚Üí        aarch64)
   539‚Üí            DOCKER_SHA256="236c5064473295320d4bf732fbbfc5b11b6b2dc446e8bc7ebb9222015fb36857"
   540‚Üí            DOCKER_ROOTLESS_SHA256="15895df8b46ff33179d357e61b600b5b51242f9b9587c0f66695689e62f57894"
   541‚Üí            SYSBOX_SHA256="6a543f863cf77cbec285f9eebbbe5d5e5c0f3fd3836347909b4ef1e4b3fc03ef"
   542‚Üí            COMPOSE_SHA256="0c4591cf3b1ed039adcd803dbbeddf757375fc08c11245b0154135f838495a2f"
   543‚Üí            ;;
   544‚Üí    esac
   545‚Üí
   546‚Üí    local DOCKER_URL="https://download.docker.com/linux/static/stable/${ARCH}/docker-${DOCKER_VERSION}.tgz"
   547‚Üí    local DOCKER_ROOTLESS_URL="https://download.docker.com/linux/static/stable/${ARCH}/docker-rootless-extras-${DOCKER_ROOTLESS_VERSION}.tgz"
   548‚Üí    local SYSBOX_URL="https://github.com/thieso2/sysbox/releases/download/v${SYSBOX_VERSION}/${SYSBOX_TARBALL}"
   549‚Üí    local COMPOSE_URL="https://github.com/docker/compose/releases/download/v${COMPOSE_VERSION}/docker-compose-linux-${ARCH}"
   550‚Üí
   551‚Üí    mkdir -p "$LOG_DIR" "$RUN_DIR" "$ETC_DIR" "$BIN_DIR"
   552‚Üí    mkdir -p "$DOCKER_DATA" "$DOCKER_CONFIG_DIR"
   553‚Üí    mkdir -p "${RUN_DIR}/containerd"
   554‚Üí    mkdir -p "$CACHE_DIR"
   555‚Üí    mkdir -p "$SYSBOX_RUN_DIR"
   556‚Üí    mkdir -p "$SYSBOX_DATA_DIR"
   557‚Üí
   558‚Üí    # Create system user and group for this instance.
   559‚Üí    # dockerd runs as root but creates the socket owned by this group (--group flag),
   560‚Üí    # so operators simply join the group to get socket access without sudo.
   561‚Üí    if ! getent group "${INSTANCE_GROUP}" &>/dev/null; then
   562‚Üí        groupadd --system "${INSTANCE_GROUP}"
   563‚Üí        echo "  Created group ${INSTANCE_GROUP}"
   564‚Üí    else
   565‚Üí        echo "  Group ${INSTANCE_GROUP} already exists"
   566‚Üí    fi
   567‚Üí    if ! getent passwd "${INSTANCE_USER}" &>/dev/null; then
   568‚Üí        useradd --system --no-create-home --shell /bin/false \
   569‚Üí            --gid "${INSTANCE_GROUP}" "${INSTANCE_USER}"

Web search results for query: "sysbox btrfs EOVERFLOW "value too large for defined data type""

Links: [{"title":"Value too large for defined data type (Solaris Common Messages and Troubleshooting Guide)","url":"https://docs.oracle.com/cd/E19455-01/806-1075/msgs-2215/index.html"},{"title":"Bug #1990849 ‚ÄúLXD containers using shiftfs on ZFS or TMPFS broke...‚ÄĚ : Bugs : linux package : Ubuntu","url":"https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux/+bug/1990849"},{"title":"[RESOLVED] Value too large for defined data type... issue - Linux Mint Forums","url":"https://forums.linuxmint.com/viewtopic.php?t=197011"},{"title":"Unable to mount - Value too large for defined data type - Help and Support - rclone forum","url":"https://forum.rclone.org/t/unable-to-mount-value-too-large-for-defined-data-type/9719"},{"title":"141167 ‚Äď find: .: Value too large for defined data type","url":"https://bugzilla.redhat.com/show_bug.cgi?id=141167"},{"title":"Value too large for defined data type on files larger than 2GB - Raspberry Pi Forums","url":"https://forums.raspberrypi.com/viewtopic.php?t=206127"},{"title":"[Overlay] Value too large for defined data type ¬∑ Issue #11700 ¬∑ moby/moby","url":"https://github.com/moby/moby/issues/11700"},{"title":"[SOLVED] Can't cp: Read error: Value too large for defined data type / Newbie Corner / Arch Linux Forums","url":"https://bbs.archlinux.org/viewtopic.php?id=230579"},{"title":"value too large for defined data type (ntfs-3g)","url":"https://forum.tinycorelinux.net/index.php/topic,23061.0.html"},{"title":"#757199 - [icedove] [32bit] getdents() = EOVERFLOW - Mail & Extensions inaccessible when profile folder resides on large XFS or BTRFS volume with 64bit inodes - Debian Bug report logs","url":"https://bugs.debian.org/cgi-bin/bugreport.cgi?bug=757199"}]

I found some relevant search results for your query. Here's what the search returned:

The search results show several instances of the "Value too large for defined data type" (EOVERFLOW) error in various contexts:

1. **Error Definition**: The symbolic name for this error is EOVERFLOW, errno=79.

2. **Sysbox-related Issue**: This issue is breaking Docker+Sysbox containers, specifically related to shiftfs on ZFS or TMPFS. Users encountered "Value too large for defined data type" errors when trying to touch files in containers using shiftfs.

3. **Btrfs and Large Inodes**: BTRFS uses 64-bit inodes by default for volumes greater than 1TiB, which can cause compatibility issues with 32-bit applications. This was specifically documented in a Debian bug report where getdents() calls failed with EOVERFLOW when accessing directories on BTRFS volumes with 64-bit inodes.

The search results suggest this error often occurs when there's a mismatch between 32-bit and 64-bit inode handling on filesystems like Btrfs, or in containerization scenarios involving Sysbox and shiftfs.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Web search results for query: "linux kernel btrfs idmapped mounts EOVERFLOW 64-bit inode"

Links: [{"title":"[v4,21/21] btrfs/242: introduce btrfs specific idmapped mounts tests - Patchwork","url":"https://patchwork.kernel.org/project/linux-btrfs/patch/20210727104900.829215-22-brauner@kernel.org/"},{"title":"[v3,21/21] btrfs/242: introduce btrfs specific idmapped mounts tests - Patchwork","url":"https://patchwork.kernel.org/project/linux-btrfs/patch/20210726102816.612434-22-brauner@kernel.org/"},{"title":"[PATCH 00/24] btrfs: support idmapped mounts","url":"https://lore.kernel.org/linux-btrfs/YO2V+n1Cttrky+bD@zeniv-ca.linux.org.uk/t/"},{"title":"[24/24] btrfs/242: introduce btrfs specific idmapped mounts tests - Patchwork","url":"https://patchwork.kernel.org/project/linux-btrfs/patch/20210713111344.1149376-25-brauner@kernel.org/"},{"title":"[v4,8/8] btrfs/244: introduce btrfs specific idmapped mounts tests - Patchwork","url":"https://patchwork.kernel.org/project/linux-btrfs/patch/20210814104805.1124023-9-brauner@kernel.org/"},{"title":"[v2,6/6] btrfs/244: introduce btrfs specific idmapped mounts tests - Patchwork","url":"https://patchwork.kernel.REDACTED.479633-7-brauner@kernel.org/"},{"title":"btrfs: support idmapped mounts [LWN.net]","url":"https://lwn.net/Articles/862737/"},{"title":"Bug #2009065 ‚Äúoverlayfs mounts as R/O over idmapped mount‚ÄĚ : Bugs : linux package : Ubuntu","url":"https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux/+bug/2009065"},{"title":"[PATCH] ksmbd: fix lookup on idmapped mounts","url":"https://lkml.kernel.org/linux-cifs/000001d794a0$94ec20a0$bec461e0$@samsung.com/T/"},{"title":"Re: [PATCH v4 00/21] btrfs: support idmapped mounts ‚ÄĒ Linux BTRFS","url":"https://www.spinics.net/lists/linux-btrfs/msg115369.html"}]

Based on the search results, I found relevant information about this topic. Here's what the search revealed:

The search results relate to btrfs-specific idmapped mounts tests that check for EOVERFLOW errors in various scenarios, including subvolume creation and renaming operations on idmapped mounts where the caller's fsids do not have mappings in the superblock.

The work on enabling idmapped mounts on btrfs was developed by Christian Brauner, and btrfs was one of the filesystems most frequently requested from userspace for this feature. The series required changes to the VFS for specific lookup helpers that btrfs relies on to perform permission checking when looking up an inode, and these changes were also required to support some other filesystems.

All non-filesystem-wide ioctls that perform permission checking based on inodes can be supported on idmapped mounts, though there are restrictions that mainly affect the deletion of subvolumes by subvolume ID, while behavior on idmapped and non-idmapped mounts is otherwise identical for all enabled ioctls.

The EOVERFLOW error appears in test cases for scenarios where idmap translations cause UID/GID values to overflow when fsids don't have proper mappings in the idmapped mount superblock.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Web search results for query: "shiftfs btrfs "value too large" inode number overflow"

Links: [{"title":"Bug #1990849 ‚ÄúLXD containers using shiftfs on ZFS or TMPFS broke...‚ÄĚ : Bugs : linux package : Ubuntu","url":"https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux/+bug/1990849"},{"title":"How can I disable shiftfs on a lxd installed from source - LXD - Linux Containers Forum","url":"https://discuss.linuxcontainers.org/t/how-can-i-disable-shiftfs-on-a-lxd-installed-from-source/15222"},{"title":"NFS: Value too large to be stored in data type","url":"https://www.ibm.com/support/pages/nfs-value-too-large-be-stored-data-type"}]

I found search results related to your query. Here's what I found:

LXD containers using shiftfs on ZFS or TMPFS broke starting with kernel 5.15.0-48.54, with users encountering "Value too large for defined data type" errors when trying to create files inside containers.

The underlying issue involves NFS assigning inode numbers larger than can be stored in a 32-bit field, which causes calls like open and opendir to return EOVERFLOW.

The search results suggest this was a known bug in Ubuntu's kernel that had a fix available through a patch for shiftfs to fix the -EOVERFLOW error inside containers.

However, the search results don't contain detailed information specifically about shiftfs with Btrfs or the exact technical details of how inode number overflow interacts with these filesystems. If you need more specific technical information about the interaction between shiftfs and Btrfs inode handling, you may need to search for kernel source code or more technical documentation on this topic.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

content.content:
# Bug #1990849 Technical Analysis

## Root Cause
The issue stems from two kernel changes introduced in version 5.15.0-48.54 that are incompatible with shiftfs:
- "fs: tweak fsuidgid_has_mapping()"
- "fs: support mapped mounts of mapped filesystems"

These changes added a check in the `may_create()` function that validates filesystem user namespace mappings.

## Mechanism of EOVERFLOW Error
When attempting file operations within an LXD container using shiftfs, the kernel's `may_create()` function performs this check:

```c
if (!fsuidgid_has_mapping(dir->i_sb, mnt_userns))
    return -EOVERFLOW;
```

The function fails to recognize valid user namespace mappings when shiftfs is layered on top of certain filesystems, causing the check to return -EOVERFLOW (error code -EOVERFLOW = "Value too large for defined data type"). This prevents basic operations like `touch` and `lxc file push` from functioning.

## Affected Filesystems
- **ZFS** (primary impact)
- **TMPFS** (also affected)

## The Fix
Aleksandr Mikhalitsyn developed a patch titled "[SAUCE] shiftfs: fix -EOVERFLOW inside the container" that addresses the incompatibility by modifying how user namespace mapping validation interacts with shiftfs mounts.

## Status
The fix was committed to:
- **Kinetic**: linux 5.19.0-34.35 (released March 2023)
- **Jammy**: linux 5.15.0-68.75 (released March 2023)
- **Lunar**: Also included in fix

All affected Ubuntu releases now show "Fix Released" status.

Here are the search results for your query about Nestybox Sysbox, shiftfs, idmapped mounts, and kernel version detection:

## Key Findings:

**Technology Overview:**
As of kernel 5.19+, ID-mapped mounts provide an almost full replacement for shiftfs. Shiftfs is a module called shiftfs that has a similar purpose to ID-mapped mounts but predates it.

**Kernel Version Support:**
- The Linux kernel 5.12 introduces a feature called "ID-mapped mounts", which is a kernel-native replacement to the shiftfs module currently used by Sysbox.
- In kernels 5.12 to 5.18, shiftfs is not required but having it causes Sysbox to setup the container's filesystem more efficiently, so it's recommended.

**Detection and Usage:**
Sysbox detects the presence of the shiftfs module and uses it when appropriate (e.g., when ID-mapped mounts are not available or can't be used on top of a particular filesystem).

Sysbox added support for ID-mapped overlayfs lower layers, which eliminates need for shiftfs and requires kernel 5.19+, and Sysbox performs shiftfs and ID-mapping functional checks during init.

**Limitations:**
ID-mapped mount mechanisms have limitations such as: can't be mounted on top of overlayfs, btrfs, or tmpfs, and overlayfs can't be mounted on top of an ID-mapped mount ext4.

I found search results related to your query. Here's what I found:

## Key Findings:

LXD containers using shiftfs on ZFS or TMPFS were broken on Ubuntu kernel version 5.15.0-48.54, with the issue being an EOVERFLOW error.

The fix was titled "[SAUCE] shiftfs: fix -EOVERFLOW inside the container", which was part of Ubuntu kernel updates.

## Related Context on Inode Number Issues:

A problem occurs with 32-bit ino_t values on 64-bit architectures (like s390x). S390x is a 64-bit arch that has a 32-bit ino_t value for historical reasons.

The solution involves using 64-bit numbers everywhere unless someone has mounted with the ino32 mount option, and only doing the conversion before presenting values to userland in getattr and readdir.

Software not built with LFS support may start failing stat() calls with -EOVERFLOW on inode numbers >2^32.

The search results show that both the ceph filesystem and shiftfs had to deal with similar EOVERFLOW issues related to inode number handling on architectures with mismatched ino_t sizes versus actual inode number widths.

content.content:
# Sysbox ID-Mapped Mounts Support

## Kernel Requirements
Linux kernel 5.12 introduced the ID-mapped mounts feature, which serves as a "kernel-native replacement to the shiftfs module currently used by Sysbox."

## Implementation Status
As of Sysbox version 0.5.0, the project now supports ID-mapped mounts.

## Known Limitations
The Linux ID-mapped mount mechanism has several constraints:

- **Cannot be mounted on**: overlayfs, btrfs, or tmpfs filesystems
- **Cannot be mounted on**: /dev/null
- **Overlayfs restriction**: Cannot be mounted on top of an ID-mapped ext4 mount

## Future Outlook
The maintainers acknowledge these limitations and note that "We expect these to be resolved in future kernel releases and we will keep an eye on these."

The documentation does not provide additional technical implementation details beyond acknowledging that ID-mapped mounts replace the previous shiftfs-based approach for Sysbox's UID/GID mapping capabilities.

# Sysbox: Shiftfs vs ID-Mapped Mounts

## ID-Mapped Mounts (Kernel 5.12+)

Sysbox leverages the Linux kernel's "ID-mapped mounts" feature beginning with version 0.5.0 to perform filesystem user-ID and group-ID mapping between containers and the host. This allows files mounted from the host to display correct ownership inside containers without manual remapping.

**Key limitation:** ID-mapped mounts cannot be applied to files or directories backed by specialized filesystems like device files at this time.

## Shiftfs Module

Ubuntu kernels include the `shiftfs` module, which serves a similar purpose to ID-mapped mounts but predates it. However, shiftfs is not standardized and remains unavailable in most Linux distributions outside Ubuntu (though it can be manually installed on Debian and Flatcar).

## Kernel Version Requirements

| Kernel Version | Shiftfs Requirement |
|---|---|
| < 5.12 | **Required** |
| 5.12‚Äď5.18 | Optional but recommended for efficiency |
| ‚Č• 5.19 | Not required |

## Selection Strategy

Sysbox automatically detects shiftfs availability and uses it "when ID-mapped mounts are not available or can't be used on top of a particular filesystem." As of kernel 5.19+, ID-mapped mounts provide "an almost full replacement for shiftfs."

**No mention exists in the documentation** of BTRFS-specific issues, EOVERFLOW errors, inode number limitations, or detailed filesystem compatibility matrices beyond the general statement about specialized filesystems.

Based on the search results, here's what I found regarding sysbox, idmapped mounts, and btrfs:

## Key Findings:

**Btrfs and ID-mapped Mounts Support:**
ID-mapped mounts can't be mounted on top of overlayfs, btrfs, or tmpfs, according to Sysbox's documented limitations. However, for Linux 5.15, Btrfs support for IDMAPPED mounts appears ready, with patches being queued into the Btrfs kernel development tree.

**ID-mapped Mounts and Bind Mounts:**
The ID-mapped mounts feature applies to all virtual filesystem (VFS) mounts, so bind mounts are included. Sysbox leverages the Linux ID-mapped mounts feature (kernel >= 5.12) or alternatively the shiftfs kernel module to ensure that host files that are bind-mounted into the container show up with proper user-ID and group-ID inside the container.

**Permission Issues:**
The search results reference "operation not permitted" errors in the context of Sysbox when ID-mapped mounts aren't supported on certain filesystems. One error message indicates "Failed to set mount attr: operation not permitted" when attempting to set up ID-mapped mounts on unsupported filesystems.

The combination of chmod, touch, EOVERFLOW, and "operation not permitted" suggests issues that could arise when attempting file operations on bind-mounted paths with ID-mapping, particularly when the underlying filesystem or kernel doesn't fully support the feature.

content.content:
# Sysbox ID-Mapped Mounts Issue Summary

## The Error

The user encountered a failure when attempting to run Docker containers with the Sysbox runtime. The error message indicated: "idmapped mounts are not supported on the filesystem at this path."

## Filesystem Details

The user initially configured storage on XFS, then reformatted to ext4 after encountering the same error. However, the problem persisted even with ext4, suggesting a kernel-level limitation rather than a filesystem type issue.

## Kernel Version

The system ran "Linux 5.14.0-70.13.1.el9_0.x86" on AlmaLinux 9.0. Notably, kernel logs displayed the message: "VFS: idmapped mount is not enabled," indicating the kernel lacked support for ID-mapped mount functionality.

## Root Cause Analysis

A maintainer of the upstream Linux kernel feature explained that "some CentOS versions have disabled Idmapped mounts." The issue wasn't about specific filesystems but rather kernel configuration on the AlmaLinux/CentOS distribution.

## Sysbox Requirements

According to the maintainer's explanation, "the Linux kernel must either support ID-mapped-mounts or support the shiftfs module" for Sysbox to function properly. These mechanisms allow host files mounted into containers to display correct user ID/group ID ownership within the container's user namespace.

For Linux 5.15 the Btrfs support for idmapped mounts appears ready. With various Btrfs changes by Canonical's Christian Brauner, the IDMAPPED mounts support is ready. With the code now being queued into the Btrfs "for-next" Git code, it makes it material for the upcoming Linux 5.15 merge window.

IDMAPPED mounts was introduced to the Linux kernel earlier this year with Linux 5.12 and allows for different mounts to expose the same file or directory with different ownership.

The search results show that Christian Brauner created patches to support idmapped mounts in btrfs, including "allow idmapped getattr iop" among other changes. # Btrfs IDMAPPED Mounts Support - Technical Details

## Timeline & Kernel Version
The feature was set to land in **Linux 5.15**. The article states that "IDMAPPED mounts support is ready" with code queued into the Btrfs "for-next" Git branch, making it "material for the upcoming Linux 5.15 merge window."

## Initial Implementation Context
IDMAPPED mounts were first introduced in Linux 5.12, initially supporting FAT and EXT4 filesystems. XFS support followed, with Btrfs joining in the 5.15 development cycle.

## Patches & Developer
The implementation involved "various Btrfs changes by Canonical's Christian Brauner," who contributed multiple patches to enable the functionality. The changes were merged into the kernel's Btrfs development tree.

## Purpose & Use Cases
The feature "allow[s] for different mounts to expose the same file or directory with different ownership." Intended applications include container environments and systemd-homed integration.

## Documentation
For comprehensive technical details, the article references Christian Brauner's "FOSDEM 2021 presentation" as the authoritative source on IDMAPPED mounts architecture and implementation.

**Note:** The article focuses on announcement rather than detailed technical specifications or known limitations.

content.text:
# Docker Named Volumes vs Bind Mounts: Practical Downsides for Sandcastle

## 1. Host Access

**Named volumes** are stored under the Docker data root at:
```
<data-root>/volumes/<volume-name>/_data/
```

For Sandcastle's Dockyard daemon, that would be:
```
/sandcastle/dockyard/lib/docker/volumes/<volume-name>/_data/
```

You **can** browse and edit files directly at that path as root. However:
- The path is not intuitive or self-documenting compared to `/sandcastle/data/users/thies/home`
- You need to know the volume name to find the path
- Docker does not guarantee this internal layout across versions (it's an implementation detail, not a contract)
- Tools like `scp` or file managers pointed at `/sandcastle/data/users/thies/home` would need to be reconfigured to point at the Docker-internal path

**With bind mounts**, the host path is explicit, predictable, and part of your design. With named volumes, you're relying on Docker's internal directory structure.

## 2. Backup/Restore

**Bind mounts:**
- Standard filesystem tools work directly: `rsync`, `tar`, `cp`, BTRFS snapshots
- Backup scripts reference well-known paths
- BTRFS subvolume snapshots are trivial: `btrfs subvolume snapshot /sandcastle/data/users/thies /snapshots/thies-2026-03-24`

**Named volumes:**
- You can still use `rsync`/`tar` on the `_data/` directory, but you're reaching into Docker internals
- BTRFS snapshots of the `_data/` directory work **only if** that directory is on a BTRFS filesystem (which it would be if the Dockyard data root is on BTRFS)
- However, you **cannot** `btrfs subvolume snapshot` a subdirectory unless it is itself a subvolume. Docker does not create individual subvolumes per named volume by default
- The idiomatic Docker way to back up a volume is to spin up a temporary container that mounts the volume and runs `tar`: `docker run --rm -v myvolume:/data -v /backup:/backup alpine tar czf /backup/myvolume.tar.gz -C /data .` -- this is cumbersome compared to direct filesystem access
- `docker volume` CLI has no built-in backup/restore commands

## 3. Migration Between Hosts

**Bind mounts:** `rsync -a /sandcastle/data/users/ newhost:/sandcastle/data/users/` -- straightforward.

**Named volumes:**
- No native `docker volume export/import` (though Docker Desktop added this, the engine CLI does not have it)
- You must either: (a) tar the `_data/` directory and untar on the new host, or (b) use a helper container
- Volume names are local to the Docker daemon -- there's no portable reference
- If moving to a host with a different Docker data root, you need to recreate volumes and copy data

## 4. Performance

On Linux with the default `local` driver, **named volumes and bind mounts have identical performance** -- both are simple directory mounts. There is no abstraction layer or copy-on-write overhead from Docker itself.

One difference: named volumes support the `nocopy` flag and Docker will auto-populate a named volume from the image contents on first use. This auto-population step is a one-time copy and generally irrelevant for Sandcastle (you're mounting over `/home/{user}` which wouldn't have image content).

**No practical performance difference.**

## 5. Permissions/Ownership

**Bind mounts:**
- The host directory's ownership and permissions are exactly what appears inside the container
- You control UIDs/GIDs from the host side
- Sysbox applies UID/GID shifting (userns remapping), so host UID 0 inside the container maps to a high UID on the host. With bind mounts on BTRFS, this is where EOVERFLOW occurs (BTRFS `chown` to UIDs > 2^31 fails on some kernels)

**Named volumes:**
- Docker can set ownership at mount time based on the image's directory permissions (first-use auto-population)
- Under Sysbox with userns remapping, named volumes are handled correctly because Sysbox manages the ID mapping at the volume level -- this is precisely why you're considering the switch
- However, if you access the `_data/` directory from the host, you'll see the **shifted** UIDs (e.g., UID 231072 instead of UID 1000), making host-side file management confusing
- Multiple containers with different userns mappings sharing the same volume would see conflicting UIDs

## 6. Multi-Container Sharing

**Both bind mounts and named volumes** can be shared between multiple containers simultaneously. Named volumes have no advantage or disadvantage here.

However, with named volumes:
- All containers sharing the volume must use the same Docker daemon
- If two containers have different userns mappings (as Sysbox containers might), UID conflicts arise
- Sandcastle currently shares `/home/{user}` across all of a user's sandboxes via bind mount. With named volumes, you'd mount the same named volume into each sandbox -- this works fine as long as all sandboxes for that user have the same UID mapping

## 7. Lifecycle Management

**Bind mounts:** The directory exists independently of any container. Creating/destroying containers has zero effect on the data. The directory lifecycle is entirely in your control.

**Named volumes:**
- Volumes persist after container removal (`docker rm`) -- they are **not** automatically deleted
- `docker rm -v` or `docker volume rm` explicitly removes them
- `docker volume prune` removes **all unused volumes** -- this is dangerous if run carelessly and could wipe user data
- `docker compose down -v` removes volumes defined in the compose file -- an accidental `-v` flag destroys data
- You must be careful with tooling and scripts to never accidentally prune or remove volumes

This is a **significant operational risk**. With bind mounts, no Docker command can delete your data. With named volumes, several common Docker commands can.

## 8. Visibility/Debugging

**Bind mounts:**
- `ls /sandcastle/data/users/thies/home` -- immediately obvious
- Any file manager, editor, or monitoring tool works directly
- `du -sh /sandcastle/data/users/*/home` gives per-user disk usage trivially

**Named volumes:**
- `docker volume ls` lists volumes but shows no size or content info
- `docker volume inspect <name>` shows the mountpoint path
- To browse contents: navigate to `/sandcastle/dockyard/lib/docker/volumes/<name>/_data/` (as root)
- `docker system df -v` shows volume sizes, but it's slow on large installations
- Debugging requires knowing Docker internals; less accessible to sysadmins unfamiliar with Docker's storage layout

## 9. BTRFS Interaction

**Where Docker stores named volumes on BTRFS:**

Docker's `local` storage driver stores volumes at `<data-root>/volumes/<name>/_data/`. Docker itself does **not** create BTRFS subvolumes for named volumes. The `volumes/` directory is a regular directory within whatever filesystem the data root sits on.

However, Docker's **graph driver** (for image/container layers) does interact with BTRFS. When Docker detects a BTRFS filesystem, it may use the `btrfs` graph driver, which creates subvolumes for layers. But this is for images and container layers, **not for named volumes**.

**Implications for Sandcastle:**
- Named volumes would sit under `/sandcastle/dockyard/lib/docker/volumes/` as regular directories on BTRFS
- You **cannot** snapshot individual named volumes as BTRFS subvolumes unless you manually create subvolumes at those paths (which Docker doesn't do and wouldn't expect)
- You lose the current per-user BTRFS subvolume benefit that `BtrfsHelper` provides
- To get BTRFS subvolume benefits with named volumes, you'd need to use Docker's volume driver API to create a custom BTRFS volume driver -- significant engineering effort

## 10. Sysbox Specifics and Dockyard

Sandcastle runs a separate Docker daemon ("Dockyard") at `/sandcastle/dockyard` with data root at `/sandcastle/dockyard/lib/docker`. Named volumes for this daemon would live at:

```
/sandcastle/dockyard/lib/docker/volumes/<volume-name>/_data/
```

Key Sysbox considerations:
- Sysbox uses **shiftfs** or **ID-mapped mounts** (kernel 5.12+) to handle userns remapping for volumes. Named volumes work well with this because Sysbox can apply the mapping cleanly at mount time
- For bind mounts, Sysbox needs the host filesystem to support the ID mapping mechanism. On BTRFS, this is where the EOVERFLOW problem occurs -- BTRFS's `chown` implementation doesn't handle the high UIDs that shiftfs/userns mapping produces
- Named volumes stored on the Docker data root may use a different filesystem layer depending on the graph driver. If Docker is using the `btrfs` graph driver, container layers are BTRFS subvolumes, but volumes are still plain directories

## Summary of Key Downsides

| Concern | Severity | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Accidental data loss (`prune`, `down -v`) | **High** | No equivalent risk with bind mounts |
| Loss of BTRFS per-user subvolumes | **High** | BtrfsHelper cannot create subvolumes inside Docker's volume directory structure |
| Host-side file access less intuitive | **Medium** | Paths are long, require root, rely on Docker internals |
| UID shifting makes host files confusing | **Medium** | Files appear owned by high UIDs on the host |
| Backup requires Docker-internal knowledge | **Medium** | No more simple `rsync` on well-known paths |
| Migration between hosts is harder | **Medium** | No portable volume export; must copy `_data/` dirs |
| No native per-volume BTRFS snapshots | **Medium** | Docker doesn't create subvolumes for named volumes |
| `docker volume prune` is a footgun | **Medium** | One command can wipe all unused user home directories |
| Debugging requires Docker knowledge | **Low** | Sysadmins need to know `docker volume inspect` |
| Performance | **None** | Identical on Linux |

## Recommendation Consideration

The EOVERFLOW problem with BTRFS + bind mounts + Sysbox userns is a real issue, but switching to named volumes trades it for: loss of BTRFS subvolume benefits, operational risk from volume lifecycle commands, and reduced host-side visibility. A middle ground worth investigating would be whether the kernel's ID-mapped mounts (available since 5.12, improved in 5.19+) can be used with BTRFS bind mounts to avoid the EOVERFLOW without switching to named volumes, or whether upgrading the kernel/BTRFS resolves the chown overflow issue directly.

toolUseResult.prompt:
Research the practical downsides of using Docker named volumes instead of bind mounts for user home directories in a container platform context.

Context: Sandcastle is a self-hosted Docker sandbox platform where each user gets a Sysbox container with SSH access. Currently, persistent home directories use bind mounts from the host BTRFS filesystem (e.g., `/sandcastle/data/users/thies/home:/home/thies`). We're considering switching to Docker named volumes because Sysbox handles them correctly (no EOVERFLOW issues with BTRFS).

Research and enumerate ALL practical downsides of Docker named volumes vs bind mounts:

1. **Host access**: Can the host directly browse/edit files in a named volume? How? What's the path?
2. **Backup/restore**: How do you back up named volumes? Can you use btrfs snapshots? rsync?
3. **Migration**: Can volumes be moved between hosts easily?
4. **Performance**: Any performance difference?
5. **Permissions/ownership**: How do permissions work differently?
6. **Multi-container sharing**: Can multiple containers share the same named volume?
7. **Lifecycle management**: What happens to the volume when the container is destroyed?
8. **Visibility/debugging**: How easy is it to inspect volume contents from the host?
9. **BTRFS interaction**: Where does Docker store named volumes on a BTRFS host? Does it use BTRFS subvolumes for them?
10. **Sysbox specifics**: Sysbox uses its own Docker data root. Where would named volumes live under a Sysbox-managed Docker daemon (Dockyard)?

The Docker daemon in question runs at /sandcastle/dockyard with data root at /sandcastle/dockyard/lib/docker. Check where named volumes would be stored under this path.

content.text:
# Docker Named Volumes vs Bind Mounts: Practical Downsides for Sandcastle

## 1. Host Access

**Named volumes** are stored under the Docker data root at:
```
<data-root>/volumes/<volume-name>/_data/
```

For Sandcastle's Dockyard daemon, that would be:
```
/sandcastle/dockyard/lib/docker/volumes/<volume-name>/_data/
```

You **can** browse and edit files directly at that path as root. However:
- The path is not intuitive or self-documenting compared to `/sandcastle/data/users/thies/home`
- You need to know the volume name to find the path
- Docker does not guarantee this internal layout across versions (it's an implementation detail, not a contract)
- Tools like `scp` or file managers pointed at `/sandcastle/data/users/thies/home` would need to be reconfigured to point at the Docker-internal path

**With bind mounts**, the host path is explicit, predictable, and part of your design. With named volumes, you're relying on Docker's internal directory structure.

## 2. Backup/Restore

**Bind mounts:**
- Standard filesystem tools work directly: `rsync`, `tar`, `cp`, BTRFS snapshots
- Backup scripts reference well-known paths
- BTRFS subvolume snapshots are trivial: `btrfs subvolume snapshot /sandcastle/data/users/thies /snapshots/thies-2026-03-24`

**Named volumes:**
- You can still use `rsync`/`tar` on the `_data/` directory, but you're reaching into Docker internals
- BTRFS snapshots of the `_data/` directory work **only if** that directory is on a BTRFS filesystem (which it would be if the Dockyard data root is on BTRFS)
- However, you **cannot** `btrfs subvolume snapshot` a subdirectory unless it is itself a subvolume. Docker does not create individual subvolumes per named volume by default
- The idiomatic Docker way to back up a volume is to spin up a temporary container that mounts the volume and runs `tar`: `docker run --rm -v myvolume:/data -v /backup:/backup alpine tar czf /backup/myvolume.tar.gz -C /data .` -- this is cumbersome compared to direct filesystem access
- `docker volume` CLI has no built-in backup/restore commands

## 3. Migration Between Hosts

**Bind mounts:** `rsync -a /sandcastle/data/users/ newhost:/sandcastle/data/users/` -- straightforward.

**Named volumes:**
- No native `docker volume export/import` (though Docker Desktop added this, the engine CLI does not have it)
- You must either: (a) tar the `_data/` directory and untar on the new host, or (b) use a helper container
- Volume names are local to the Docker daemon -- there's no portable reference
- If moving to a host with a different Docker data root, you need to recreate volumes and copy data

## 4. Performance

On Linux with the default `local` driver, **named volumes and bind mounts have identical performance** -- both are simple directory mounts. There is no abstraction layer or copy-on-write overhead from Docker itself.

One difference: named volumes support the `nocopy` flag and Docker will auto-populate a named volume from the image contents on first use. This auto-population step is a one-time copy and generally irrelevant for Sandcastle (you're mounting over `/home/{user}` which wouldn't have image content).

**No practical performance difference.**

## 5. Permissions/Ownership

**Bind mounts:**
- The host directory's ownership and permissions are exactly what appears inside the container
- You control UIDs/GIDs from the host side
- Sysbox applies UID/GID shifting (userns remapping), so host UID 0 inside the container maps to a high UID on the host. With bind mounts on BTRFS, this is where EOVERFLOW occurs (BTRFS `chown` to UIDs > 2^31 fails on some kernels)

**Named volumes:**
- Docker can set ownership at mount time based on the image's directory permissions (first-use auto-population)
- Under Sysbox with userns remapping, named volumes are handled correctly because Sysbox manages the ID mapping at the volume level -- this is precisely why you're considering the switch
- However, if you access the `_data/` directory from the host, you'll see the **shifted** UIDs (e.g., UID 231072 instead of UID 1000), making host-side file management confusing
- Multiple containers with different userns mappings sharing the same volume would see conflicting UIDs

## 6. Multi-Container Sharing

**Both bind mounts and named volumes** can be shared between multiple containers simultaneously. Named volumes have no advantage or disadvantage here.

However, with named volumes:
- All containers sharing the volume must use the same Docker daemon
- If two containers have different userns mappings (as Sysbox containers might), UID conflicts arise
- Sandcastle currently shares `/home/{user}` across all of a user's sandboxes via bind mount. With named volumes, you'd mount the same named volume into each sandbox -- this works fine as long as all sandboxes for that user have the same UID mapping

## 7. Lifecycle Management

**Bind mounts:** The directory exists independently of any container. Creating/destroying containers has zero effect on the data. The directory lifecycle is entirely in your control.

**Named volumes:**
- Volumes persist after container removal (`docker rm`) -- they are **not** automatically deleted
- `docker rm -v` or `docker volume rm` explicitly removes them
- `docker volume prune` removes **all unused volumes** -- this is dangerous if run carelessly and could wipe user data
- `docker compose down -v` removes volumes defined in the compose file -- an accidental `-v` flag destroys data
- You must be careful with tooling and scripts to never accidentally prune or remove volumes

This is a **significant operational risk**. With bind mounts, no Docker command can delete your data. With named volumes, several common Docker commands can.

## 8. Visibility/Debugging

**Bind mounts:**
- `ls /sandcastle/data/users/thies/home` -- immediately obvious
- Any file manager, editor, or monitoring tool works directly
- `du -sh /sandcastle/data/users/*/home` gives per-user disk usage trivially

**Named volumes:**
- `docker volume ls` lists volumes but shows no size or content info
- `docker volume inspect <name>` shows the mountpoint path
- To browse contents: navigate to `/sandcastle/dockyard/lib/docker/volumes/<name>/_data/` (as root)
- `docker system df -v` shows volume sizes, but it's slow on large installations
- Debugging requires knowing Docker internals; less accessible to sysadmins unfamiliar with Docker's storage layout

## 9. BTRFS Interaction

**Where Docker stores named volumes on BTRFS:**

Docker's `local` storage driver stores volumes at `<data-root>/volumes/<name>/_data/`. Docker itself does **not** create BTRFS subvolumes for named volumes. The `volumes/` directory is a regular directory within whatever filesystem the data root sits on.

However, Docker's **graph driver** (for image/container layers) does interact with BTRFS. When Docker detects a BTRFS filesystem, it may use the `btrfs` graph driver, which creates subvolumes for layers. But this is for images and container layers, **not for named volumes**.

**Implications for Sandcastle:**
- Named volumes would sit under `/sandcastle/dockyard/lib/docker/volumes/` as regular directories on BTRFS
- You **cannot** snapshot individual named volumes as BTRFS subvolumes unless you manually create subvolumes at those paths (which Docker doesn't do and wouldn't expect)
- You lose the current per-user BTRFS subvolume benefit that `BtrfsHelper` provides
- To get BTRFS subvolume benefits with named volumes, you'd need to use Docker's volume driver API to create a custom BTRFS volume driver -- significant engineering effort

## 10. Sysbox Specifics and Dockyard

Sandcastle runs a separate Docker daemon ("Dockyard") at `/sandcastle/dockyard` with data root at `/sandcastle/dockyard/lib/docker`. Named volumes for this daemon would live at:

```
/sandcastle/dockyard/lib/docker/volumes/<volume-name>/_data/
```

Key Sysbox considerations:
- Sysbox uses **shiftfs** or **ID-mapped mounts** (kernel 5.12+) to handle userns remapping for volumes. Named volumes work well with this because Sysbox can apply the mapping cleanly at mount time
- For bind mounts, Sysbox needs the host filesystem to support the ID mapping mechanism. On BTRFS, this is where the EOVERFLOW problem occurs -- BTRFS's `chown` implementation doesn't handle the high UIDs that shiftfs/userns mapping produces
- Named volumes stored on the Docker data root may use a different filesystem layer depending on the graph driver. If Docker is using the `btrfs` graph driver, container layers are BTRFS subvolumes, but volumes are still plain directories

## Summary of Key Downsides

| Concern | Severity | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Accidental data loss (`prune`, `down -v`) | **High** | No equivalent risk with bind mounts |
| Loss of BTRFS per-user subvolumes | **High** | BtrfsHelper cannot create subvolumes inside Docker's volume directory structure |
| Host-side file access less intuitive | **Medium** | Paths are long, require root, rely on Docker internals |
| UID shifting makes host files confusing | **Medium** | Files appear owned by high UIDs on the host |
| Backup requires Docker-internal knowledge | **Medium** | No more simple `rsync` on well-known paths |
| Migration between hosts is harder | **Medium** | No portable volume export; must copy `_data/` dirs |
| No native per-volume BTRFS snapshots | **Medium** | Docker doesn't create subvolumes for named volumes |
| `docker volume prune` is a footgun | **Medium** | One command can wipe all unused user home directories |
| Debugging requires Docker knowledge | **Low** | Sysadmins need to know `docker volume inspect` |
| Performance | **None** | Identical on Linux |

## Recommendation Consideration

The EOVERFLOW problem with BTRFS + bind mounts + Sysbox userns is a real issue, but switching to named volumes trades it for: loss of BTRFS subvolume benefits, operational risk from volume lifecycle commands, and reduced host-side visibility. A middle ground worth investigating would be whether the kernel's ID-mapped mounts (available since 5.12, improved in 5.19+) can be used with BTRFS bind mounts to avoid the EOVERFLOW without switching to named volumes, or whether upgrading the kernel/BTRFS resolves the chown overflow issue directly.

ID-mapped mounts can't be mounted on top of overlayfs, btrfs, or tmpfs, which is a significant limitation of this Linux kernel feature.

ID-mapped mounts don't work in kernel 5.13 when BTRFS is the underlying filesystem, which historically motivated the continued use of shiftfs even after ID-mapped mounts were introduced.

content.content:
# Sysbox Shiftfs Mounting Issue #376

## Problem
Sysbox incorrectly mounts shiftfs on bind-mounted directories even when unnecessary. The issue occurs when a host directory's UID/GID falls within the mapped range for the container's user namespace.

## Technical Details

**Symptom:** A bind-mounted directory owned by UID 166536 (sysbox:165536 + 1000) appears as "nobody:nobody" inside the container.

**Root Cause:** The system mounted shiftfs unnecessarily. As stated in the issue: "The problem is in sysbox-runc; the solution is to ensure it does not mount shiftfs on a host dir that is bind-mounted into the sysbox container if the uid:gid of that host dir falls within the range of uid:gids mapped to the sysbox container."

**Evidence:** Output from `findmnt` showed:
```
/mnt/testdir  /var/lib/sysbox/shiftfs/[uuid]  shiftfs rw,relatime
```

## Resolution
The issue was resolved through pull requests in sysbox-runc. The fix ensures that shiftfs mounting is skipped when the host directory's ownership already matches the container's mapped UID/GID range, eliminating unnecessary filesystem overlay operations.

# Technical Analysis: Sysbox Shiftfs Mount Issue in AKS

## The Problem
Users running Sysbox CE 0.4.1 in AKS with Docker-in-Docker (DinD) encountered persistent volume issues after multiple disk detach/reattach cycles. The error "value too large for defined data type" appeared when Docker tried to `chmod /var/lib/docker`.

## Root Cause Analysis

The issue stems from a cascade of failures in Sysbox's uid-shifting logic:

1. **Initial Failure**: When a container stops, Sysbox fails to revert uid-shifts on the mount source, leaving directory ownership at a container's assigned uid (e.g., 558752:558752) rather than reverting it.

2. **State Corruption**: This orphaned ownership persists on the PVC across pod recreation cycles since the same Azure Disk reattaches to the same StatefulSet pod.

3. **UID Mismatch**: When a new pod instance starts, it receives a different uid assignment (e.g., 427680:427680). Sysbox detects the mismatch between the volume's existing ownership and the container's assigned uid.

4. **Incorrect Mount Decision**: Rather than adjusting ownership, Sysbox incorrectly mounts shiftfs on `/var/lib/docker`‚ÄĒa decision that should never occur for bind-mounted volumes destined for Docker's storage directory.

## Shiftfs Interaction

Shiftfs is a kernel filesystem that performs uid/gid translation. When mounted on `/var/lib/docker`, it causes permission complications, particularly when Docker attempts filesystem operations like `chmod`. The mount point becomes owned by "nobody" and "nogroup," breaking Docker daemon functionality.

## The Fix Gap

The developers identified two underlying issues requiring fixes:

- Why the "failed to revert uid-shift" error occurs during container shutdown
- Whether Sysbox should automatically adjust volume ownership when detecting uid mismatches between volumes and new containers

The investigation revealed the problem as transient‚ÄĒmanifesting after multiple attach/detach cycles rather than on initial deployment.

# Technical Issue Summary: Sysbox Runtime Failure on Ubuntu Kernel 5.15.0-48

## Affected Kernel Versions
- **Broken:** Ubuntu 5.15.0-48 and later (5.15.0-52 confirmed affected)
- **Working:** 5.15.0-47 and earlier, kernel 5.19+, kernel 6.0.0+

## Error Details
**Error Code:** `EOVERFLOW` ("value too large for defined data type")

The failure occurs during container initialization: "failed to mkdirall /var/lib/sysbox/shiftfs/[UUID]/var/lib/rancher: mkdir: value too large for defined data type"

## Root Cause
Ubuntu kernel 5.15.0-48 introduced changes that broke shiftfs compatibility with overlayfs. Specifically, a change in overlayfs/super.c replaced `mnt_user_ns(path->mnt) != &init_user_ns` with `is_idmapped_mnt(path->mnt)`, affecting how ID-mapped mounts interact with shiftfs. The kernel apparently lost Ubuntu-specific patches required for overlayfs-shiftfs interaction.

## Filesystem Technology
- **shiftfs:** Ubuntu-specific kernel module for user namespace ID shifting (never officially upstreamed)
- **overlayfs:** Used by Docker for container rootfs layers
- **Alternative:** ID-mapped mounts (kernel 5.19+ supports overlayfs compatibility)

## Solutions

**Permanent fixes:**
- Upgrade to kernel 5.19, 6.0.0, or later
- Downgrade to kernel 5.15.0-47 or earlier

**Workarounds for 5.15.0-48+:**
1. Disable shiftfs via systemd override: `--disable-shiftfs` flag
2. Remove shiftfs module: `rmmod shiftfs`

When shiftfs is disabled, sysbox falls back to ID-mapped mounts with a `chown` workaround for overlayfs compatibility.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about the Linux kernel features you queried:

## Overview

The MOUNT_ATTR_IDMAP flag can be set via the mount_setattr() syscall together with a file descriptor referring to a user namespace, and the user namespace referenced by the namespace file descriptor will be attached to the bind mount.

## Btrfs Support

Btrfs is among the filesystems that support ID-mapped mounts. The search results show that Btrfs has been updated to handle idmapped mounts with functions like i_gid_into_mnt() that validate group IDs and can return -EOVERFLOW.

## Bind Mounts

ID-mapped mounts apply to all virtual filesystem (VFS) mounts, so bind mounts are included.

## EOVERFLOW Error

The search results mention EOVERFLOW in the context of ID mapping validation. If any user IDs or group IDs are not mapped, all files owned by that unmapped user or group ID will appear as being owned by the overflow user ID or overflow group ID respectively.

The results I found reference the foundational work from 2020 (kernel patches) and general documentation. However, I did not find specific recent discussions (2024-2025) about particular EOVERFLOW issues with btrfs bind mounts using mount_setattr and MOUNT_ATTR_IDMAP in these results. Based on the search results, here's what I found regarding your query about btrfs, 64-bit inode numbers, shiftfs, EOVERFLOW, getdents, stat, 32-bit containers, and user namespaces:

## Key Technical Issues

XFS uses 64-bit inodes by default for volumes >1TiB (and BTRFS does now, too), which creates compatibility problems with 32-bit applications.

If the 32 bit version of stat() and readdir calls is used on a filesystem with 64 bit inodes, then the call may fail with EOVERFLOW. The failure will happen only if the particular inode number cannot be expressed in 32 bits.

## The Core Problem

There is no kernel API which can be used on the host to say "give me inodes and offsets which will fit into a 32 bit field". The 'getdents' syscall uses the "unsigned long" type for the d_ino and d_off fields, so on a 64-bit host these will be the same size as the ino64_t and off64_t used by 'getdents64'.

## Btrfs-Specific Solutions

Setting inumbits=64 minimizes the chances of duplicate inode numbers, but a lower number (such as 56) may make sense in situations (such as when overlayfs is in use) where the top bits are used by other subsystems.

This issue is particularly relevant to containerized environments where 32-bit applications or user namespace containers interact with btrfs filesystems that support 64-bit inode numbers, as there's a fundamental incompatibility between the data structures available to 32-bit getdents/stat syscalls and the actual inode values returned by modern filesystems.

content.content:
# Btrfs Inode Number Problem: Technical Analysis

## Core Problem

Btrfs uses **64-bit inode numbers**, but these are allocated **per-subvolume** rather than globally across the filesystem. This creates collisions when subvolumes are exposed via NFS or overlayfs, since these systems expect unique inode identifiers within a mounted filesystem.

## The 64-Bit Constraint

The article doesn't specify an exact inode range, but notes that 64 bits are shared between multiple purposes. As one commenter observed: "64-bit inodes already give you room for ~18 quintillion inodes per filesystem," yet the real issue isn't bit quantity‚ÄĒit's allocation strategy across subvolume boundaries.

## Interaction with Overlayfs

The piece explains overlayfs's workaround: "it over-loads the high bits and hopes the filesystem doesn't use them." This fragile approach reserves bits from the 64-bit space for overlay-specific purposes, reducing available bits for actual inode identification.

## Proposed Solutions

**Take 4 (Final Approach):** Btrfs provides a "uniquifier" value‚ÄĒa combined subvolume and inode hash using XOR operations‚ÄĒthat NFS server code uses internally without exposing it to clients. This requires no user-space API changes.

## Notable Omissions

The article contains **no mention** of EOVERFLOW, shiftfs, idmapped mounts, or user namespaces‚ÄĒsuggesting these aren't central to the core inode collision problem being addressed.

# Technical Root Cause Analysis

## The Core Issue

The bug report documents a compatibility problem between 32-bit applications and filesystems using 64-bit inode numbers. The mechanism is straightforward:

**The Problem:** The 32-bit version of Icedove/Thunderbird uses the legacy `getdents()` system call, which relies on `struct dirent` structures designed for 32-bit inode values. When the filesystem (XFS or BTRFS) returns directory entries containing 64-bit inode numbers that exceed the 32-bit field capacity, the kernel returns `EOVERFLOW` rather than truncating the data.

**Why It Happens:** As documented in the report, XFS automatically enables 64-bit inodes for volumes exceeding 1 TiB. The strace output shows the call failing: `"getdents(24, 0xab44401c, 32768) = -1 EOVERFLOW"` when operating on such volumes, while succeeding on ext4 with 32-bit inodes.

## The Solution

The report indicates that **64-bit Thunderbird/Icedove versions work correctly** because they use `getdents64()` or modern directory iteration methods that handle 64-bit inode numbers.

## Kernel Version Information

The document does not specify which kernel version introduced fixes for this issue. It only notes that Mozilla fixed a related 32-bit `stat()` family issue "4 years ago" (relative to August 2014), suggesting awareness of these compatibility constraints existed in the early 2010s.

The fundamental incompatibility stems from architecture-level limitations rather than a patchable kernel bug.

## Filesystem Support and Detection

ID-mapped mounts have limitations and can't be mounted on top of overlayfs, btrfs, or tmpfs. This is significant because it means sysbox-runc must detect when these filesystems are in use and fall back to alternative mechanisms.

## ID-Mapped Mounts vs Shiftfs

Sysbox detects the presence of the shiftfs module and uses it when appropriate (e.g., when ID-mapped mounts are not available or can't be used on top of a particular filesystem). As of kernel 5.19+, ID-mapped mounts provide an almost full replacement for shiftfs.

## Bind Mount Handling

When creating a bind-mount into a container, Sysbox will use ID-mapped-mounts or shiftfs to ensure the bind-mounted file shows up with proper ownership inside the rootless Sysbox container.

## Filesystem Limitations

For kernels >= 5.12 and < 6.3, installing shiftfs is recommended but not required, as these kernels support ID-mapped mounts, though it doesn't work with all filesystems yet (e.g., works with ext4 but not tmpfs).

The search results show that sysbox-runc includes logic in its `prepBindMounts` function to detect which filesystem is backing a bind mount source and decide whether to use ID-mapped mounts or fall back to shiftfs based on filesystem compatibility.

Based on the provided documentation, here's what the document reveals about Sysbox's bind mount approach:

## Supported Mechanisms

The document describes two primary technologies Sysbox uses for bind mounts:

1. **ID-mapped mounts** (kernel >= 5.12): "leverages the Linux ID-mapped mounts feature (kernel >= 5.12)"

The documentation explains that these mechanisms ensure "host files that are bind-mounted into the container show up with proper user-ID and group-ID inside the container."

## Fallback Behavior

When neither technology is available, the document states: "if neither ID-mapped mounts or shiftfs are present in your host, then host files mounted into the Sysbox container will show up as owned by `nobody:nogroup` inside the container."

The search has returned results related to your query. Here's what I found:

## Key Findings:

**Generic VFS getattr and stat handling:**
The Linux VFS generic_fillattr function handles idmapped mounts by taking care to map the inode according to the idmap before filling in the uid and gid fields. The function directly assigns the inode number to the stat structure with `stat->ino = inode->i_ino`.

**Btrfs 64-bit inode numbers:**
Btrfs inode numbers that can be assigned to user created files are from the whole 64-bit space except the first 256 and last 256 that are reserved for internal b-tree identifiers.

**EOVERFLOW error with 64-bit inodes:**
If the 32-bit version of stat or readdir calls is used on a filesystem with 64-bit inodes, the call may fail with EOVERFLOW. The failure will happen only if the particular inode number cannot be expressed in 32 bits.

**Idmapped mounts with Btrfs:**

input.prompt: Extract the technical root cause: what causes 32-bit fstat to return EOVERFLOW when st_ino is too large? What is the exact kernel code path? What kernel version fixed this?

input.query: sysbox-fs fuse inode number btrfs 64-bit ino stat EOVERFLOW "value too large"

content.content:
# Technical Root Cause Analysis

## The Core Problem

When 32-bit applications run on 64-bit kernels without the `-D_FILE_OFFSET_BITS=64` flag, they encounter an EOVERFLOW error from `fstat()`. As explained in the bug report:

The kernel declares `ino_t` as `"unsigned long"`, which is 32-bit on ia32 but 64-bit on x86_64. When a 32-bit app calls `fstat()` on a 64-bit kernel, the kernel returns inode numbers that can exceed 32-bit capacity. The userspace glibc function `__xstat32_conv()` attempts to fit this 64-bit value into the app's 32-bit `st_ino` field. When the inode number is too large, "glibc allocates a stat64 struct to hold the data from the fstat64 call" but cannot safely convert it, triggering EOVERFLOW.

## Kernel Code Path & Fix

The problematic code involved the `new_inode()` and `iunique()` functions, which use a static `last_inode` counter declared as `"unsigned long"`. 

**The fix**: Change this counter to `"unsigned int"` when `CONFIG_COMPAT` is enabled. As one commenter noted: "if 32-bits is enough on a 32-bit kernel, it should be enough on a 64-bit kernel."

## Resolution Version

The bug was fixed in **RHEL3 U9 (kernel version 2.4.21-47.4.EL)** and formally released in advisory **RHSA-2007-0436** (closed June 11, 2007).

Based on the web search results, I found information related to your query about EOVERFLOW errors with 64-bit inode numbers. Here are the relevant findings:

## Key Issues Found:

The search results reveal a common problem where 64-bit file IDs are served by certain filesystems by default, and unless 32-bit applications allow for compilation with "largefile" support, they don't know how to properly handle stat()/lstat() calls against 64-bit file-id-having files.

Even when calling lstat64(), glibc attempts to convert the result of the 64-bit call into the 32-bit fileid stat structure, and it can't do so without losing information, resulting in an EOVERFLOW error.

## Related Filesystem Examples:

On XFS with inode64 mode and other clustered filesystems that use 64-bit inodes like CephFS, getdents() or fstat64() can fail with EOVERFLOW errors when returning 64-bit inodes.
